brief_document.title: Implement signal delivery via typed channels
brief_document.purpose: Signal delivery to running workflows through typed liminal channels. Each running workflow gets a dedicated signal channel (ADR-002 typed channels) created at workflow start and torn down at completion. External clients and other workflows deliver signals by publishing to the workflow's signal channel. Payloads are validated against declared signal types at publish time (not consume time), catching schema mismatches before they reach the workflow. The workflow process receives signals as standard BEAM messages, integrating with Aion's existing selective-receive signal handling without modification. Signal channels are ephemeral by default; for workflows that need signal replay during recovery, the channel can be configured as durable with recorder capture.
brief_document.task: Create signal.rs in crates/liminal/src/aion/. This module handles signal channel lifecycle (create on workflow start, teardown on completion/termination), publish-time payload validation against declared signal types, delivery to the workflow process as BEAM messages, and optional durable mode for signal replay during recovery. Use the channel naming from AION-001 channels.rs (signal_channel constructor). Signal delivery integrates with Aion's existing selective-receive signal handling -- this module does NOT modify that handling, it feeds signals into it through the channel. This brief does NOT implement signal routing across namespaces or signal type declaration (those are Aion-side concerns).

requirements.title: Per-workflow signal channel creation
requirements.spec: WHEN a workflow starts, THE SYSTEM SHALL create a signal channel named by signal_channel(namespace, workflow_id) from channels.rs. The channel SHALL be typed with the workflow's declared signal types per ADR-002. WHEN the channel already exists (workflow restart/recovery), THE SYSTEM SHALL reuse the existing channel. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT create signal channels for workflows that declare no signal handlers.
acceptance: Starting a workflow with signal handlers creates a channel named aion.signal.{namespace}.{workflow_id}
Starting a workflow without signal handlers does not create a signal channel
Restarting a workflow that already has a signal channel reuses the existing channel (no duplicate, no error)
The created channel is typed with the workflow's declared signal type set

requirements.title: Publish-time payload validation
requirements.spec: WHEN an external client or another workflow publishes a signal to a workflow's signal channel, THE SYSTEM SHALL validate the signal payload against the workflow's declared signal types before accepting the publish. IF the payload does not match any declared signal type, THE SYSTEM SHALL reject the publish with Err(AionSurfaceError::SignalValidationFailed) containing the signal name, expected types, and actual content-type. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT defer validation to consume time -- invalid signals never enter the channel.
acceptance: Publishing a SignalPayload with signal_name matching a declared type and valid payload bytes succeeds
Publishing a SignalPayload with signal_name not in the declared type set returns Err(AionSurfaceError::SignalValidationFailed)
Publishing a SignalPayload with correct signal_name but wrong content_type returns Err(AionSurfaceError::SignalValidationFailed)
The error includes the signal_name, the expected content_type, and the actual content_type
An invalid signal never appears in the channel's message stream

requirements.title: Delivery as BEAM messages
requirements.spec: WHEN a valid signal is published to a workflow's signal channel, THE SYSTEM SHALL deliver the signal to the workflow process as a standard BEAM message. The delivery SHALL integrate with Aion's existing selective-receive signal handling -- the workflow process receives signals through its normal mailbox, not a separate callback. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT modify Aion's selective-receive signal handling mechanism.
acceptance: A published signal arrives in the workflow process's mailbox as a BEAM message
The delivered message contains the signal_name and payload from the original SignalPayload
The workflow's existing selective-receive code can pattern-match on the delivered signal without modification
grep -rn 'selective.receive' signal.rs returns no matches that modify the existing mechanism (read-only references allowed for documentation)

requirements.title: Channel teardown on workflow completion
requirements.spec: WHEN a workflow completes (Completed, Failed, Cancelled, or TimedOut status), THE SYSTEM SHALL tear down the workflow's signal channel. WHEN a signal is published to a torn-down channel, THE SYSTEM SHALL return Err(AionSurfaceError::SignalDeliveryFailed) indicating the workflow is no longer running. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT leave orphaned signal channels for terminated workflows.
acceptance: After a workflow completes with any terminal status, its signal channel no longer exists
Publishing to a signal channel after workflow completion returns Err(AionSurfaceError::SignalDeliveryFailed)
The error message includes the workflow_id and indicates the workflow is terminated
No signal channels remain for workflows in terminal states after teardown (no orphans)

requirements.title: Optional durable mode for signal replay
requirements.spec: WHEN a signal channel is configured as durable, THE SYSTEM SHALL capture signal deliveries as recorded events in the workflow's event log via the recorder. WHEN the resolver replays a workflow, THE SYSTEM SHALL return the recorded signal deliveries without re-executing the actual channel publish. WHEN a signal channel is ephemeral (default), THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT record signal deliveries -- signals are delivered live only.
acceptance: A durable signal channel records each signal delivery as an event in the workflow's event log
During replay of a workflow with durable signals, the resolver returns recorded signals without channel interaction
An ephemeral signal channel (default) does not write signal events to the recorder
The durable/ephemeral choice is per-workflow configuration, not global

boundaries: SHALL NOT modify Aion's existing selective-receive signal handling -- signals are fed into it, not replacing it.
SHALL NOT implement signal routing across namespaces -- namespace isolation is preserved.
SHALL NOT implement signal type declaration -- the workflow's declared types come from Aion's workflow definition.
SHALL NOT implement signal ordering guarantees beyond per-causal-chain ordering provided by liminal channels (ADR-005).
SHALL NOT implement activity dispatch -- that is AION-002.
verification: cargo test for the signal module passes (unit tests for channel creation, validation rejection, teardown, durable replay).
No file in crates/liminal/src/aion/ exceeds 500 lines.
Publishing an invalid signal payload is rejected at publish time with a descriptive error (not silently dropped, not deferred to consume time).
No orphaned signal channels remain after workflow termination (verified by test that terminates a workflow and asserts channel does not exist).

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

adrs.title: Typed channels with compile-time enforcement in Gleam and runtime validation for external producers
adrs.decision: Every channel declares its message type. Gleam producers get compile-time enforcement. External producers (Python, TypeScript, HTTP) get runtime validation at publish time. The rejected alternative is the NATS model: channels carry opaque bytes and consumers handle deserialization failures.

checklist.text: Per-workflow signal channel created on workflow start via channels.rs naming convention aion.signal.{namespace}.{workflow_id}.
Signal payloads validated against the workflow's declared signal types at publish time, not consume time.
Workflow process receives signals as standard BEAM messages through its signal channel subscription.
Signal channel torn down when workflow completes or is terminated.

stories.text: As the Aion Engine, I want to deliver signals to running workflows through typed liminal channels so that signal delivery is validated and integrated with the same channel infrastructure used for activity dispatch.
As a Workflow Author, I want signals delivered reliably to my workflow so that I can react to external events with confidence that validated signals arrive in order.

constraints.text: No file exceeds 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic, no type definitions, no function bodies.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Activity dispatch must complete through a liminal conversation, never by direct function call to a worker. The conversation mediates lifecycle, failure, and recording.
All non-deterministic channel operations must be recorder-compatible: the recorder can capture them and the resolver can replay them without re-executing the actual dispatch.
Channel names follow the aion.{kind}.{namespace}.{id} convention enforced by the channels module. No ad-hoc string construction of channel names outside that module.
Worker failure detection uses beamr process links exclusively. No heartbeat timers, no health-check polling, no gRPC keepalive.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, Aion dispatches activities, delivers signals, and streams workflow history entirely through liminal conversations and channels. The gRPC worker dispatch layer is gone. A workflow author writes schedule_activity and the bus handles routing, failure detection, and re-dispatch. An operator scales the worker pool by starting or stopping worker processes -- no rebalancing, no configuration changes, no downtime. The zero-hop embedded path means a beamr instance running both Aion and liminal dispatches activities without crossing a network boundary, and the same code path works identically when workers run on remote nodes connected via beamr distribution.

This is the cluster that proves the layer cake: haematite stores durable state, liminal mediates communication, Aion orchestrates workflows. If this integration surface is clean, every future consumer of liminal (the messaging product, the real-time channels product) inherits the same patterns.
